title: Compare areas by multiplying
description: Compare the areas of rectangles represented in images or contexts.

assessment_items.question_content:
A rocket ship has $3$ rectangular windows, shown in the following table.  

 | Side length $1$ | Side length $2$
- | :-: | :-:
Front window | $7 \text { m}$ | $6 \text { m}$   
Side window | $4 \text { m}$| $4 \text { m}$   
Back window | $8 \text { m}$ | $5 \text { m}$   

**Which window has the smallest area?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Front window:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD {7} \times\blueD{6}\\
&=\blueD {42}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Side window:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\redD{\text{Area}}&=\redD {4} \times\redD{4}\\\\
&=\redD {16}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Back window:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD{8} \times\greenD{5}\\\\
&=\greenD{40}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

We can list them from largest area to smallest area.

Front window: $\blueD{42}\text{ square m}$  
Back window: $\greenD{40}\text{ square m}$    
Side window: $\redD{16}\text{ square m}$

$\blueD{42}\text{ square m} > \greenD{40}\text{ square m} > \redD{16}\text{ square m}$
The side window has the smallest area.

choices.content: Front window
Side window
Back window

assessment_items.question_content:
Li Min measured $3$ of her rectangular classrooms and recorded their lengths in the following table.   

 | Side length $1$ | Side length $2$
- | :-: | :-:
Art classroom | $10 \text { m}$ | $7 \text { m}$   
Math classroom | $6 \text { m}$| $9 \text { m}$   
Science classroom | $8 \text { m}$ | $8 \text { m}$   

**Which classroom has the smallest area?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Art classroom:

[[☃ image 1]]
  
$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD {10} \times\blueD{7}\\
&=\blueD {70}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Math classroom:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\redD{\text{Area}}&=\redD {6} \times\redD{9}\\
&=\redD {54}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Science classroom:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD {8} \times\greenD{8}\\
&=\greenD {64}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

We can list them from largest area to smallest area.

Art classroom: $\blueD{70}\text{ square m}$  
Science classroom: $\greenD {64}\text{ square m}$  
Math classroom: $\redD {54}\text{ square m}$

$\blueD{70}\text{ square m} > \greenD{64}\text{ square m} > \redD{54}\text{ square m}$
The math classroom has the smallest area.

choices.content: Art classroom
Math classroom
Science classroom

assessment_items.question_content:
Davi, Gustavo, and Mariana each pack a rectangular blanket for their picnic. The measurements of their blankets are listed in the following table.

| Side length $1$ | Side length $2$
- | :-: | :-:
Davi's blanket | $4 \text { m}$ | $2 \text { m}$   
Gustavo's blanket | $3 \text { m}$| $4 \text { m}$   
Mariana's blanket | $3 \text { m}$ | $3 \text { m}$   

**If they want to use the blanket with the largest area for their picnic, which blanket should they choose?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Davi:

[[☃ image 1]]  

$\begin{align}\redD{\text{Area}}&=\redD {4} \times\redD{2}\\
&=\redD {8}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Gustavo:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD {3} \times\blueD{4}\\
&=\blueD {12}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Mariana: 

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD {3} \times\greenD{3}\\
&=\greenD {9}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

We can list them from largest area to smallest area.

Gustavo: $\blueD {12}\text{ square m}$  
Mariana: $\greenD {9}\text{ square m}$  
Davi: $\redD {8}\text{ square m}$

$\blueD{12}\text{ square m} > \greenD{9}\text{ square m} > \redD{8}\text{ square m}$
Gustavo's blanket has the largest area.

choices.content: Davi's blanket
Gustavo's blanket
Mariana's blanket

assessment_items.question_content:
A movie theater is showing movies on $3$ different rectangular screens.  The side lengths of the screens playing each movie are listed in the following table.

Movie | Side length $1$ | Side length $2$
- | :-: | :-:
Dino Dig | $10 \text { m}$ | $8 \text { m}$   
Monster Mania | $9 \text { m}$| $8 \text { m}$   
Wiggle Worms | $10 \text { m}$ | $9 \text { m}$   

**Which movie is playing on the screen with the largest area?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Dino Dig:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD {10} \times\blueD{8}\\
&=\blueD {80}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Monster Mania:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\redD{\text{Area}}&=\redD {9} \times\redD{8}\\
&=\redD {72}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Wiggle Worms:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD {10} \times\greenD{9}\\
&=\greenD {90}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

We can list them from largest area to smallest area.

Wiggle Worms: $\greenD{90}\text{ square m}$  
Dino Dig: $\blueD{80}\text{ square m}$  
Monster Mania: $\redD {72}\text{ square m}$

$\greenD{90}\text{ square m} > \blueD{80}\text{ square m} > \redD{72}\text{ square m}$
The screen for Wiggle Worms has the largest area.

choices.content: Dino Dig
Monster Mania
Wiggle Worms

assessment_items.question_content:
James has a striped and a polka dotted piece of wrapping paper (shown below).  

Striped:  

[[☃ image 1]]

Polka dotted:  

[[☃ image 2]]

**If James wants to use the piece with the larger area, which wrapping paper should he pick?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Striped:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD{6} \times\greenD{3}\\
&=\greenD{18}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Polka dotted:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD{4} \times\blueD{4}\\
&=\blueD{16}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Now, we can compare the areas. 

$\greenD{18}\text{ square m}>\blueD{16}\text{ square m}$
The striped piece of wrapping paper has the larger area.

options.alt: A rectangle with stripes has its left side labeled 3 meters and its top side labeled 6 meters.
A rectangle with polka dots has its left side labeled 4 meters and its top side labeled 4 meters.

choices.content: Striped
Polka dotted
Both rectangles have the same area.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rafaela has two magic flying carpets: a striped one and a polka dotted one (shown below). 

Striped:  

[[☃ image 1]]

Polka dotted:  

[[☃ image 2]]

**If Rafaela wants to fly the carpet with the smaller area, which carpet should she pick?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Striped:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\maroonD{\text{Area}}&=\maroonD{6} \times\maroonD{6}\\\\
&=\maroonD{36}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Polka dotted:

[[☃ image 1]]
  
$\begin{align}\purpleD{\text{Area}}&=\purpleD{7} \times\purpleD{5}\\\\
&=\purpleD{35}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Now, we can compare the areas. 

$\maroonD{36}\text{ square m}>\purpleD{35}\text{ square m}$
The polka dotted magic flying carpet has the smaller area.

options.alt: A square with stripes has its left side labeled 6 meters and its top side labeled 6 meters.
A rectangle with polka dots has its left side labeled 5 meters and its top side labeled 7 meters.

choices.content: Striped
Polka dotted
Both magic carpets have the same area.

assessment_items.question_content:
Searching Shreya and Traveling Tim are setting out to explore with their treasure maps (shown below).  

Searching Shreya:  

[[☃ image 1]]

Traveling Tim:  

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which treasure map has the larger area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Searching Shreya:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}}&=\blueD{7} \times\blueD{10}\\
&=\blueD{70}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Traveling Tim:

[[☃ image 1]]
  
$\begin{align}\purpleD{\text{Area}}&=\purpleD{9} \times\purpleD{8}\\
&=\purpleD{72}\text{ square m}\end{align}$

Now, we can compare the areas. 

$\blueD{70}\text{ square m}<\purpleD{72}\text{ square m}$
Traveling Tim's treasure map has the larger area.

options.alt: A rectangle with the left side labeled 7 meters and the top side labeled 10 meters.
A rectangle with the left side labeled 8 meters and the top side labeled 9 meters.

choices.content: Searching Shreya's treasure map
Traveling Tim's treasure map
Both treasure maps have the same area.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jada and Vitor create rectangular bookmarks (shown below). 

Jada:  

[[☃ image 1]]

Vitor:  

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which bookmark has the larger area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

Jada:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}}&=\greenD{4} \times\greenD{12}\\\\
&=\greenD{48}\text{ square cm}\end{align}$

Vitor:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}\redD{\text{Area}}&=\redD{6} \times\redD{8}\\\\
&=\redD{48}\text{ square cm}\end{align}$

Now, we can compare the areas. 

$\greenD{48}\text{ square cm}=\redD{48}\text{ square cm}$
Both bookmarks have the same area.

options.alt: A rectangle with the left side labeled 12 centimeters and the top side labeled 4 centimeters.
A rectangle with the left side labeled 8 centimeters and the top side labeled 6 centimeters.

choices.content: Jada's bookmark
Vitor's bookmark
Both bookmarks have the same area.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which rectangle has the greatest area?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We can find the area of a rectangle by multiplying its two side lengths.

[[☃ image 1]]
  
$\begin{align}\greenD{\text{Area}} &=\greenD {10} \times \greenD4\\
&= \greenD {40} \text { square cm}\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]
    
$\begin{align}\blueD{\text{Area}} &=\blueD 8 \times \blueD6\\
&= \blueD{48} \text { square cm}\end{align}$

Now, we can compare the areas. 

$\greenD{40}\text{ square cm}<\blueD{48}\text{ square cm}$

This rectangle has the greatest area:  

[[☃ image 1]]

choices.content: ![A rectangle with the left side labeled 4 centimeters and the top side labeled 10 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7e6a0438fe3104f35ac259d54817419b0860ce0c)
![A rectangle with the left side labeled 6 centimeters and the top side labeled 8 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e1b388a66a63d8422df4270c02eeadf1e5d29f7d)
Both rectangles have the same area.
